coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots based FRET for the detection of DNA damage
coredata.dc:creator: Kudr, Jiri
Richtera, Lukas
Xhaxhiu, Kledi
Hynek, David
Heger, Zbynek
Zitka, Ondrej
Adam, Vojtech
coredata.dc:description: Here, we aimed our attention at the synthesis of carbon dots (C-dots) with the ability to interact with DNA to suggest an approach for the detection of DNA damage. Primarily, C-dots modified with amine moieties were synthesized using the one-step microwave pyrolysis of citric acid in the presence of diethylenetriamine. The C-dots showed strong photoluminescence with a quantum yield of 4%. In addition, the C-dots (2.8±0.8nm) possessed a good colloidal stability and exhibited a positive surface charge (ζ=36mV) at a neutral pH. An interaction study of the C-dots and the DNA fragment of λ bacteriophage was performed, and the DNA binding resulted in changes to the photoluminescent and absorption properties of the C-dots. A binding of the C-dots to DNA was also observed as a change to DNA electrophoretic mobility and a decreased ability to intercalate ethidium bromide (EtBr). Moreover, the Förster (or fluorescence) resonance energy transfer (FRET) between the C-dots and EtBr was studied, in which the C-dots serve as an excitation energy donor and the EtBr serves as an acceptor. When DNA was damaged using ultraviolet (UV) radiation (λ=254nm) and hydroxyl radicals, the intensity of the emitted photoluminescence at 612nm significantly decreased. The concept was proved on analysis of the genomic DNA from PC-3 cells and DNA isolated from melanoma tissues.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biosensing
Fluorescence resonance energy transfer
Quantum dots
UV irradiation

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Ministry of Education, Youth and Sports of the Czech Republic

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors wish to express their thanks to Iveta Triskova, Ph.D. (Department of Chemistry, Masaryk University, Brno, Czech Republic); Monika Stupavska, Ph.D. (Department of Physical Electronics, Masaryk University, Brno, Czech Republic); and Filip Mravec, Ph.D. (Laboratory of Biotechnology and Biomaterials Research, Brno University of Technology, Brno, Czech Republic) for their expert assistance with circular dichroism analysis, XPS analysis and time-resolved fluorescence analysis, respectively. This research has been financially supported by the Ministry of Education, Youth and Sports of the Czech Republic under the project CEITEC 2020 (LQ1601).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The scheme of C-dots synthesis (modified from (Zhu et al., 2013)); (B) the C-dots aqueous solution (10µgml−1) under UV irradiation (λex=312nm) and (C) its UV/Vis absorption spectra; (D) the fluorescence emission spectra of the C-dots water solution measured at different excitation wavelengths (320–410nm) and (E) the dependence of emission maxima on the excitation wavelength; (F) the zeta potential of the C-dots within the range of pH 2–10 and (G) the C-dots’ size distribution in water; and (H) the TEM image and (I) the FT-IR spectra of the C-dots.

(A) The dependence of the C-dots (1.25µgml−1) fluorescence intensity at 450nm on DNA concentration (0–30µgml−1) and the detail of lower concentrations in the inset; (B) changes in the absorption spectra of the C-dots solution (25µgml−1) containing DNA (10µgml−1) at different times of incubation (5–60min) and changes at 266 and 350nm in the inset; (C) the gel electrophoresis of the samples containing DNA (10µgml−1) and the specific amount of C-dots (0–100µgml−1); (D) the fluorescence spectra (λex=360nm) of the C-dots solution (1.25µgml−1) containing DNA (0.5µgml−1) and EtBr bromide (0.1μgml−1), the solution containing DNA (0.5µgml−1) and EtBr (0.1μgml−1), and the solution containing C-dots (1.25µgml−1) and EtBr (0.1μgml−1); and (E) the fluorescence spectra (λex=520nm) of the C-dots solution (1.25µgml−1) containing DNA (0.5µgml−1) and EtB (0.1μgml−1), the solution containing DNA and EtBr (0.1μgml−1), and the solution containing C-dots (1.25µgml−1) and EtBr (0.1μgml−1).

(A) The fluorescence spectra of the C-dots solution (1.25µgml−1) containing DNA (0.5µgml−1) and EtBr (0.1μgml−1), the C-dots solution (1.25µgml−1) containing dsDNA (0.5µgml−1) and EtBr (0.1μgml−1) excited at 360nm, and the fluorescence spectra of the DNA solution (0.5µgml−1) containing EtBr (0.1μgml−1) and dDNA containing EtBr (0.1μgml−1) excited at 520nm; (B) the fluorescence spectra (excitation at 360nm) of the C-dots solution (1.25µgml−1) containing DNA (0.5µgml−1) damaged by UV radiation (254nm) for a specific time (0–20min) and EtBr (0.1μgml−1); (C) the fluorescence intensities at 612nm of the solution containing C-dots (1.25µgml−1), DNA (0.5µgml−1) damaged by UV radiation for a specific time (0–20min) and EtBr (0.1μgml−1), and the fluorescence intensities of the solution containing DNA (0.5µgml−1) damaged by UV irradiation for a specific time (0–20min) and EtBr (0.1μgml−1); and (D) the fluorescence spectra of the C-dots solution (1.25µgml−1) containing EtBr (0.1μgml−1) and DNA (0.5µgml−1) damaged by the Fenton reaction.

(A) The procedure for the preparation of samples for the detection of UV damage on PC-3 cell line genomic DNA; (B) the fluorescence spectra of the C-dots solution (1.25µgml−1) containing EtBr (0.1μgml−1) and DNA isolated from the PC-3 cells (0.5µgml−1) damaged by UV radiation (254nm) for 0, 1 and 5; and (C) the comparison of EtBr emissions at 612nm for samples containing melanoma and non-melanoma DNA (0.5µgml−1), C-dots (1.25µgml−1) and EtBr (0.1μgml−1).

head.ce:title: Carbon dots based FRET for the detection of DNA damage

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Biochemistry, Mendel University in Brno, Zemedelska 1, Brno CZ-613 00, Czechia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Biochemistry, Mendel University in Brno

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Central European Institute of Technology, Brno University of Technology, Technicka 3058/10, CZ-616 00 Brno, Czechia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Central European Institute of Technology, Brno University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Natural Sciences, University of Tirana, Blv. Zog I, No. 2/1, 1001 Tirana, Albania

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Natural Sciences, University of Tirana

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemistry and Biochemistry, Mendel University in Brno, Zemedelska 1, 613 00 Brno, Czechia.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Here, we aimed our attention at the synthesis of carbon dots (C-dots) with the ability to interact with DNA to suggest an approach for the detection of DNA damage. Primarily, C-dots modified with amine moieties were synthesized using the one-step microwave pyrolysis of citric acid in the presence of diethylenetriamine. The C-dots showed strong photoluminescence with a quantum yield of 4%. In addition, the C-dots (2.8±0.8nm) possessed a good colloidal stability and exhibited a positive surface charge (ζ=36mV) at a neutral pH. An interaction study of the C-dots and the DNA fragment of λ bacteriophage was performed, and the DNA binding resulted in changes to the photoluminescent and absorption properties of the C-dots. A binding of the C-dots to DNA was also observed as a change to DNA electrophoretic mobility and a decreased ability to intercalate ethidium bromide (EtBr). Moreover, the Förster (or fluorescence) resonance energy transfer (FRET) between the C-dots and EtBr was studied, in which the C-dots serve as an excitation energy donor and the EtBr serves as an acceptor. When DNA was damaged using ultraviolet (UV) radiation (λ=254nm) and hydroxyl radicals, the intensity of the emitted photoluminescence at 612nm significantly decreased. The concept was proved on analysis of the genomic DNA from PC-3 cells and DNA isolated from melanoma tissues.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon-based nanomaterials have been attracting increasing attention since their discovery (Dong et al., 2015; Jovanovic et al., 2015; Nguyen et al., 2015a; Qin et al., 2015; Sun et al., 2015). Among others, quasi-spherical nanocrystals of less than 10nm called carbon dots (C-dots), a few layers of graphene with a larger size around several tens of nm (graphene quantum dots) and nanodiamonds are the rising stars in the aforementioned field due to their unique optical and electronic properties. Although all these materials exhibit photoluminescence in the visible part of the spectrum, they differ in several characteristics such as shape, carbon hybridization, degree of crystallinity and fluorescence origin (Georgakilas et al., 2015; Zheng et al., 2015).

C-dots consist of an amorphous to nanocrystalline core with mostly sp2 hybridized carbon and have highly abundant oxygen in their structure (Baker and Baker, 2010). Their size and surface passivation influence the absorption and fluorescent characteristics; however, they harvest light in the ultraviolet (UV) region (Eda et al., 2010; Jaiswal et al., 2012; Wang and Hu, 2014). C-dots, in contrast to semiconductor quantum dots, possess several merits such as low cost, simple synthesis, versatile surface modifications, excellent photostability, small size, chemical inertness, water solubility, etc. (Jaiswal et al., 2012; Wang and Hu, 2014). These extraordinary properties, together with biocompatibility and low toxicity, make them an ideal candidate for biomedical, biochemical and chemical applications (Daia et al., 2014; Hu et al., 2014; Nguyen et al., 2016).

The interactions of nanomaterials with biomolecules are of great interest due to the aforementioned applications (Hu et al., 2014; Remya et al., 2014). From this point of view, nanoparticles’ surface chemistry (exterior functionality) controls their behaviour towards the outer environment and can lead to predictable behaviour in biointerfaces (Tmejova et al., 2015; Tonga et al., 2014). In this field, modifiers, which are able to interact with nucleic acids as branched polyethyleneimine (PEI), can be used to modify the C-dots’ surface in order to facilitate an electrostatic interaction between the negative phosphate backbone of DNA and the surface of the C-dots (Dou et al., 2015; Liu et al., 2012). In addition, Feng et al. (2013) demonstrate that C-dots synthesized from D-glucose and spermine are able to induce a right-handed B-DNA to left-handed Z-DNA transition under a physiological salt condition.

It follows on from our results that a simple biosensing device monitoring DNA and its structural changes can be suggested, due to the fact that DNA represents the UV radiation chromophore with the greatest biological importance. It is clear that the exposure of DNA to UV radiation causes several types of DNA damage; for instance, the introduction of cyclobutane pyrimidine dimers (CPDs) from two adjacent pyrimidine bases (Mouret et al., 2006). These pre-mutagenic DNA photolesions are the primary cause of skin cancers if they remain unrepaired (Lima-Bessa and Menck, 2005).

In this paper, we synthesize and characterize C-dots modified with amino moieties in order to provide them with a positively charged surface. Furthermore, we test the C-dots to determine whether they exhibit electrostatic interactions with DNA molecules. Finally, the fluorimetric method, based on Förster (or fluorescence) resonance energy transfer (FRET), is applied between C-dots and ethidium bromide (EtBr) in order to distinguish between native and damaged DNA.

Citric acid (CA), diethylenetriamine (DETA), ultrapure water and other chemicals were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St Louis, MO, USA) of American Chemical Society (ACS) purity, unless noted otherwise.

Photoluminescent C-dots with their surface modified with amine moieties were synthesized using the microwave-assisted pyrolysis of CA in the presence of DETA. Briefly, 1.921g of anhydrous CA was dissolved in 5ml of ultrapure water in a flat-bottom flask (50ml). Subsequently, DETA (1080µl) was added to the CA solution and stirred for 10min. The beaker containing the transparent solution was placed on the rotation plate of a microwave oven (700W) and heated for 3min in a row. The colour-changed, foamy solid was dissolved in ultrapure water (10ml) and the resulting solution was centrifuged (2500g, 5min) in order to remove any large carbon residuals. The supernatant was dialyzed using a low-molecular-weight cut-off membrane (Mw=1kDa) against distilled water for 5 days, with the water changed every 12h. Finally, the solution was lyophilized for 24h, and the obtained tawny solid (1.830g) showed excellent dispersibility in water.

Absorption spectra were recorded using the UV/Vis spectrophotometer Specord 210 (Analytik Jena, Jena, Germany) with quartz cuvette (Hellma, Essex, UK) at 20°C. The florescence spectra and DNA concentrations were acquired using an Infinite 200 PRO multimode reader (Tecan, Männedorf, Switzerland). Flat-bottom, UV-transparent, 96-well plates (Corning, New York, NY, USA) were used for the fluorescence measurements and a NanoQuant plate was used for the determination of the DNA concentration. A volume of 100µl of sample was pipetted into each well.

PC-3 cells were grown in Ham's F12 medium using 10% foetal bovine serum supplemented with penicillin (100Uml−1) and streptomycin (0.1mgml−1). After reaching a confluence of ~80%, the cells were detached from the Petri dish using trypsin (0.25%) and centrifuged (100g, 10min). The pellet was then re-suspended in 100µl of phosphate-buffered saline (PBS), mixed with 300µl of lysis buffer (Roche, Basel, Switzerland) in an Eppendorf tube and incubated for 30min on a roller incubator. Subsequently, 400µl of the lysate was pipetted into the sample tube that is a component of the MagNA Pure Nucleic Acid Isolation Kit I (Roche, Basel, Switzerland). The isolation steps were performed according to the manufacturer's instructions.

The tissue specimens were collected from Melanoma-bearing Libechov Minipigs (MeLiM) and provided as a gift from Dr Vratislav Horak (Institute of Animal Physiology and Genetics, Academy of Sciences, Czech Republic). After developing the spontaneous melanomas, specimens of malignant and non-malignant tissues were collected and frozen in liquid nitrogen prior to DNA isolation. Specimens M2 and L201 were collected from 22- and 12-week-old individuals respectively. The tissues used in this study were mechanically homogenized and the DNA was isolated using the procedure previously described.

The experiments with damaged DNA were performed as follows. A DNA water solution (1.0µgml−1) in a microcentrifuge tube was placed in a transilluminator TFX-35. MC (Vilber Lourmat, Eberhardzell, Germany) and was exposed to UV radiation at 254nm (70% of power) for specific time intervals. Subsequently, C-dots were added to the DNA solution to produce 0.5µgml−1 of DNA and 1.25µgml−1 of C-dots. After incubation, EtBr was added to reach a concentration of 0.1μgml−1.

The DNA damaged by radicals was prepared as follows. To the DNA solution (0.5µgml−1) containing FeCl2·4H2O (2µgml−1) different amounts of H2O2 were added to attain final concentrations of 0; 7; 17 and 35µgml−1. After incubation (15min) the samples were filtered using centrifugal filters, as described in the previous paragraph. The prepared DNA samples were mixed with C-dots and EtBr, as is also described in previous paragraph.

The DNA and C-dots were mixed together to achieve a final concentration of 10µgml−1 of DNA and specific concentrations of C-dots. Then, they were electrophoresed in 2% (m/V) agarose gel (Mercury, San Diego, CA, USA) in 1× TAE buffer (40mM Trizma-base, 20mM acetic acid and 1mM ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid, pH=8.0). The electrophoresis (Bio-Rad, Hercules, CA, USA) was run at 60V and 6°C for 160min. The gel was stained with EtBr and visualized using a transilluminator at 312nm. The Quick-load 100bp DNA Ladder (New England Biolabs, Ipswich, MA, USA) was used as a marker.

Since the discovery of C-dots in 2004, they have been broadly studied for their applications in various fields, including (bio)sensing and imaging (Cao et al., 2007; Chen et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2011). Various materials have been suggested as sources of carbon and synthetic methods (Hu et al., 2015; Li et al., 2015; Nguyen et al., 2015b; Zheng et al., 2009). Here, we used a one-step synthesis of C-dots achieved by the carbonization of CA in the presence of DETA. The preparation of the C-dots from CA using microwave hydrothermal pyrolysis is a relatively simple procedure, which has previously been reported several times (Bhaisare et al., 2015; Pierrat et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2014). Amine-moiety-rich molecules such as 1,2-ethylenediamine, spermine or polyethyleneimine were previously used to stabilize the C-dots’ surface (Feng et al., 2013; Liu et al., 2012; Zhai et al., 2012; Zhu et al., 2013). DETA was chosen as the surface passivation agent in order to obtain C-dots with a positive surface charge and to promote their interaction with the negatively charged backbone of the DNA. Zhu et al. (2013) suggest that the synthetic reaction starts with the condensation of CA and amine, whereupon polymer-like C-dots are created and, finally, carbonized into C-dots, as is shown in Fig. 1A. The result of our synthetic procedure was dialyzed and lyophilized. The tawny, solid powder obtained (yield ca. 62%) exhibited no fluorescence under UV irradiation. As prepared, the C-dots exhibited excellent water-solubility due to the presence of polar functional groups on the surface and emitted blue luminescence under UV irradiation after dissolution (Fig. 1B). Quinine sulphate was selected as a standard sample to calculate the quantum yield (QY) of the C-dots, which was found to be 4%.

In general, N-doping and N-containing passivation agents are being used in order to increase the C-dots QY; nevertheless, the origin of such a high QY for the C-dots prepared from CA and specific amines has still not been elucidated. Krysmann et al. (2012) illustrate that the temperature during pyrolysis strongly influences the final result. According to their experiments, the condensation of CA with ethanolamine at 180°C resulted in highly fluorescent C-dots’ molecular precursors, but increasing the reaction temperatures caused the synthesis of fluorescent C-dots to the detriment of the precursors. Zhai et al. (2012) highlight the fact that different amine molecules can lead to different N-doping and N-content, and hence to different photoluminescent characteristics. The time for pyrolysis was also recognized as an important factor influencing the resultant C-dots QY, since the C-dots’ formation, surface passivation and nanoparticle growth take place simultaneously, and have no well-defined boundary (Pierrat et al., 2015; Zhai et al., 2012). We attributed the relatively low QY of our C-dots to the prolonged time of dialysis with frequent changes of water, which possibly resulted in the loss of the smaller C-dots and fluorescent molecules with a higher QY (see Kasprzyk et al., 2015).

Fig. 1C shows the UV/Vis absorption spectra of the C-dots. The C-dots have two common local absorption maxima at 266nm and 450nm. The former band can be assigned to the π-π transition of C˭C bonds and the latter to the n-π transition of C˭O. Excitation-dependent photoluminescent behaviour typical for fluorescent carbon materials was observed (Fig. 1D) and is in strong agreement with previously published findings (Cai et al., 2015; Pierrat et al., 2015; Zheng et al., 2013). The highest emission intensity was observed at an excitation wavelength of 360nm (Fig. 1E). The full width at half maximum (FWHM) was 82nm using an excitation at 360nm, suggesting a narrow size distribution of C-dots.

Furthermore, we characterized the properties of C-dots as zeta potentials ζ, which were determined under different pH conditions (pH 2–10) in order to reveal the particles’ colloidal stability and their surface charge, and confirm any possible electrostatic attraction between C-dots and DNA. The C-dots demonstrate pH-dependent behaviour (Fig. 1F). Between pH 3 and 5, the C-dots possessed a surface charge higher than +55mV, which suggests a good colloidal stability. Above pH 5, the particles’ zeta potential decreased; however, up until pH 7, the particles had still moderate stability (ζ>+40mV). From pH 7 to pH 10, the C-dots’ zeta potential rapidly decreased to a value of −43mV at pH 10 (the isoelectric point was found at pH 8.8). The influence of the solution's pH revealed an obvious effect on the dispersion state of the particles and their electrostatic interactions. Moreover, this measurement enabled us to determine the pH at which C-dots possess a sufficiently high surface charge to prevent aggregation. Therefore, further experiments were performed using water as a solvent.

The average C-dots size and size distribution were obtained using dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements and are displayed in Fig. 1G. This demonstrated that the average C-dots’ size (hydrodynamic diameter) is 2.8±0.8nm at a neutral pH. Fig. 1H shows the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of the C-dots, which confirmed the presence of C-dots without apparent aggregation (see also Fig. S1). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra were recorded in order to identify the functional groups on the C-dots’ surface (Fig. 1I). The C-dots exhibited broad peaks around 3400cm−1, corresponding to the O-H vibrations. Peaks at 3240cm−1, 1641cm−1, 1353cm−1 and 725cm−1 were assigned to a secondary amine, amide C˭O, C-N stretching and N-H wagging, respectively. Furthermore, a peak at 1534cm−1, indicating the presence of amide N-H bending vibration, was observed. The full comparison of DETA, CA and C-dots FT-IR spectra is illustrated in Fig. S2. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) survey spectra of C-dots disclosed three peaks, which were assigned to O1s, N1s and C1s (Fig. S3). This data indicates that the C-dots were mainly composed of O, N and C. The XPS spectrum of C-dots in the C1s region revealed the presence of C˭O, C-O, C-N, C-C and C˭C (Fig. S4).

C-dots and DNA interaction studies were performed using fluorescence spectroscopy, UV/Vis spectrophotometry and gel electrophoresis. Since the most important property of C-dots is their fluorescence, we examined the intensities of the C-dots’ fluorescence in the presence of different amounts of DNA ( Fig. 2A). A small amount of added DNA resulted in a decrease in the C-dots’ fluorescence intensities. The fluorescence emission maxima decreased until the weight ratio of C-dots and DNA reached 2.5:1. On the contrary, a further increase in the amount of DNA added resulted in a fluorescence increase.

In the next step, the character of C-dots’ quenching by DNA was examined. According to Lakowicz (Lakowicz, 2006), a careful examination of the absorption spectra of fluorophore is among the suitable methods for the calculation of the Stern-Volmer constant for distinguishing between static and dynamic quenching. Collisional (dynamic) quenching affects the excited states of the fluorophores only; no changes in the absorption spectra are expected. The absorption spectra of C-dots (5µgml−1) and DNA (12.5µgml−1) were summed and subtracted from the absorption spectra of C-dots and DNA mixed for a certain time. Increasing absorption at 266nm and decreasing absorption at 350nm were observed in this time (inset in Fig. 2B). It is worth noting that both wavelengths represent the absorption peaks of the C-dots’ solution. Moreover, two isosbestic points at 260nm and 308nm were observed. The static quenching of C-dots by DNA was also confirmed by fluorescence lifetime measurements (Fig. S5) – no significant changes in spectra were observed. The fluorescence lifetime (weighted average of four lifetimes) of the C-dots sample was 12.8ns and decreased to 11.9ns after interaction with the DNA fragment (the absorption spectra of both samples are shown in Fig. S6).

An investigation of the interactions between the C-dots and DNA using gel electrophoresis revealed a shifting of the DNA band with the increasing concentration of C-dots, and smears at a high C-dots’ concentration were observed (Fig. 2C). Higher concentrations of applied C-dots also caused a decreasing intensity of the bands and an increasing amount of DNA also got stuck at the starting position of the gel (Fig. S7). A determination of the denaturation temperatures of DNA influenced by the C-dots showed no changes in comparison with DNA alone (Fig. S8). To confirm the binding of C-dots to DNA, we performed the commonly used competitive binding assay (Geng et al., 2013; Ramana et al., 2016). The amount of EtBr, which significantly increases its fluorescence after binding to DNA, was optimized (Fig. S9). Steady state fluorescence intensity measurements revealed DNA full saturation with EtBr at a base pairs and EtBr ratio of 4:1, which is more or less consistent with the previously reported data (Heller and Greenstock, 1994). EtBr is a well-known intercalator, which binds to the hydrophobic interior of DNA, where it is protected against quenching by molecular oxygen and water molecules. The fluorescence intensity of EtBr increases 20 times when intercalated due to the absorption of energy from excited nucleotides (Sambrook and Russell, 2001). Our results showed that C-dots can significantly influence the interaction between EtBr and DNA. The fluorescence spectrum of the DNA/C-dots complex exposed to EtBr was compared with the spectra of the DNA, with the intercalated EtBr and C-dots solution containing EtBr as the controls. At first, we used an excitation wavelength of 360nm. In comparison with the controls, the sample containing the DNA/C-dots complex with intercalated EtBr exhibited a lower fluorescence intensity for the C-dots (as described previously) and a higher fluorescence intensity of EtBr (Fig. 2D). This data suggests that a portion of the emitted energy from the C-dots is transferred to the EtBr via FRET and enhances the EtBr fluorescence. Although the donor of the energy (C-dots) and the acceptor (EtBr) need to be in nanoscale proximity to perform FRET, we conclude that C-dots must be bound to the DNA. Subsequently, the excitation wavelength optimal for EtBr excitation (520nm) was used. The sample containing EtBr and C-dots exhibited a low fluorescence, since no DNA was presented within the sample and prevented the EtBr from quenching (Fig. 2E). Both samples containing DNA exhibited an enhanced fluorescence of the EtBr; however, the sample with C-dots showed a lower fluorescence intensity. We presume that it is caused by steric protection of the EtBr binding sites by the C-dots. A similar shielding of the EtBr binding sites by C-dots modified with Polyethylene glycol (PEG) was previously observed by Milosavljevic et al. (2015).

The amount of sun UV radiation that reaches the Earth's surface is steadily increasing due to the depletion of the ozone layer (Madronich et al., 1998). DNA represents one of the key biological targets of UV radiation, which introduces several mutagenic lesions, such as CPDs and 6-4 pyrimidine-pyrimidone (6-4PP) photoproducts, into DNA (Friedberg et al., 2006). Although strong distortions of DNA caused by the presence of 6-4PP are effectively recognized and repaired by nucleotide excision repair (NER) enzymes, the mild distortions caused by CPDs are poorly repaired and can lead to cancer (Lima-Bessa and Menck, 2005; Mitchell, 1988). Several appealing methods were developed in order to detect DNA mutations; however, the identification of chemically mutated DNA containing basic sites, methylations or photodimers is challenging (Schuster, 2008).

DNA possesses a main absorption peak at 260nm, which corresponds with the π–π* transitions of nucleobases. Damaged DNA was prepared using UV irradiation at 254nm and the damage was monitored using UV/Vis spectrophotometry (Fig. S10). An increasing amount of damaged DNA sites was observed with the decrease of absorbance at 260nm and the creation of local absorption maxima at 330nm, which is connected with 6-4PP formations (Madsen et al., 2016; Oladepo and Loppnow, 2010). Among others, fluorescence-based techniques were previously reported in order to quantify DNA damage (El-Yazbi and Loppnow, 2014). Rogers et al. (1999) demonstrate that DNA damage can be quantified using thermal denaturation analysis coupled with double-strand-specific dye PicoGreen. A similar method that utilizes PicoGreen is also suggested by Cosa et al. (2002), who measured the ratio between ssDNA and dsDNA using fluorescence lifetimes.

Here, the FRET between the C-dots and EtBr was used to quantify the UV damage to the DNA. C-dots with a positive surface charge were attached to DNA and served as a donor of excitation energy. EtBr, the photophysical properties of which change dramatically when intercalated, was the energy acceptor.

We examined the sensitivity of EtBr to DNA damaged by UV radiation for 20min (λ=254nm). As can be seen in Fig. 3A, the damaged DNA complex with EtBr exhibited a fluorescence at 612nm (λex=520nm), which is 33% lower than the control sample containing non-damaged DNA. Probably, it is the consequence of a decrease in the total amount of canonical base pairs, which are partially replaced by UV photoproducts. These photoproducts are not able to accommodate EtBr or shield the intercalated EtBr from quenching. We compared these results with changes in EtBr emission intensity, when the FRET between the C-dots and EtBr took place (λex=360nm). In this case, the EtBr emission intensity decreased by 50%, which suggested that the FRET-based mechanism between C-dots and EtBr is more sensitive to UV-induced damage to DNA than EtBr alone. It is worth noting that the C-dots and EtBr were always mixed with previously damaged DNA, since it is known that dyes, and possibly also C-dots, can lower the formation of DNA lesions (Cariello et al., 1988).

The suggested mechanism was closely examined in experiments where DNA was irradiated for different times (0–20min). As is shown in Fig. 3B, the emission maxima at 612nm of EtBr decreased with an increased time of DNA irradiation (DNA damage). A detailed comparison of the DNA damage detected using the suggested mechanism and EtBr is revealed in Fig. 3C. EtBr is able to exhibit differences between intact and highly damaged DNA (after 10 and 20min of UV irradiation). However, the decrease in the EtBr emission signal is significant from the first minute of irradiation using C-dots/EtBr FRET; hence, the proposed FRET-based mechanism exhibits a higher sensitivity for DNA damage detection.

DNA damage by UV radiation is caused by the direct absorption of photons (direct damage), as studied previously in this paper, or indirectly as a consequence of free-radical production. The radiolysis of water induces hydrogen peroxide in biological systems, which serves as a common hydroxyl radical precursor and causes oxidative DNA damage. Hydrogen peroxide and other reactive oxygen species (ROS) are also products of a normal cellular metabolism and, despite a cell-antioxidant defence system, their effects are enhanced by the aforementioned UV radiation. The Fenton reaction represents an important metal-mediated pathway for hydroxyl radical generation from hydrogen peroxide; nevertheless, the biological significance of hydroxyl radicals under physiological conditions is not precisely clear (Kakhlon and Cabantchik, 2002). Hydroxyl radicals are able to interact with all DNA components and induce damage to bases as well as base-free sites’ strand breaks (Valko et al., 2004). Therefore, we decided to test Fenton chemistry to produce hydroxyl radicals in the DNA solution and induce DNA damage. Various concentrations of hydrogen peroxide were added to a DNA solution containing Fe(II) as a catalyst. C-dots and EtBr were added to dialyzed samples of the damaged DNA in an optimized concentration. As it can be seen in Fig. 3E, the EtBr emission maximum decreased with an increase in the hydrogen peroxide concentration and corresponds with an increase in DNA damage.

Since the proposed mechanism was shown to perform well with a DNA fragment (498bp), we also examined it with genomic DNA. Genomic DNA from human prostate cancer cell line PC-3 was isolated and exposed to UV at 254nm for 1 and 5min. The procedure for the preparation of these samples is shown in Fig. 4A. As it was presumed, the emission signal of EtBr at 612nm decreased with an increased time of UV irradiation (Fig. 4B). However, in comparison with the DNA fragment, the emission decrease in the case of damaged genomic DNA was mild (14% after 5min of irradiation).

The proposed mechanism for DNA damage sensing was also tested using genomic DNA isolated from MeLiM pigs’ tissues (DNA from melanoma tissue that was not additionally damaged), and compared with the performance of the sensor with DNA isolated from the control benign tissue regions from the same animals. In comparison with the DNA isolated from healthy tissue, as it can be considered to be less damaged, the fluorescence intensity of EtBr from the complex of melanoma DNA, C-dots and EtBr significantly decreased (Fig. 4C). It is worth noting that melanomas are notorious for alterations in DNA repair systems and the subsequent accumulation of DNA damage, which leads to melanomagenesis (Rass and Reichrath, 2008). DNA damage is one of the major hallmarks of cancer development that affects the cell-cycle control checkpoints (p53, Cdc2, BRCA and others) (Malumbres and Barbacid, 2009), and thus decides the cell's fate (malignant transformation or death). Indeed, our sensor confirmed pronounced differences between DNA originating from malignant and benign skin tissues. Hence, we anticipate that our designated sensor could be helpful for a simple biosensing of various kinds of potential DNA-damaging agents.

FRET is a broadly used analytical strategy due to its high sensitivity. Among others, FRET-based signal transduction is widely used in DNA hybridization sensors, where a DNA molecule often plays the role of a spacer in complex constructs (Heger et al., 2015; Zhou et al., 2015). We have prepared amine-functionalized C-dots by the one-step microwave pyrolysis of CA in the presence of DETA. The resultant fluorescent nanoparticles (2.8±0.8nm) possessed good colloid stability. The positive surface charge at a neutral pH was mediated by the stabilization of the C-dots’ surface by amine moieties proved by attenuated total reflection FT-IR. An interaction study of the aforementioned C-dots and DNA fragment was performed and suggested a high affinity of the C-dots to DNA. DNA affected the fluorescence and absorption spectra of the C-dots, and these results were confirmed by the changed electrophoretic mobility of the DNA and a competitive binding assay. Furthermore, it was shown that C-dots decrease the ability of DNA to accommodate EtBr and prevent it from solution quenching. However, C-dots are able to transfer a portion of excitation energy to EtBr and the FRET was observed. The fluorescence of EtBr enhanced by the FRET from C-dots was sensitive to the DNA state, and was able to easily distinguish native DNA from DNA damaged by UV radiation and free radicals. We anticipate that the suggested mechanism can serve as a part of biosensors suitable for monitoring of the threat originating from increased UV levels.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors wish to express their thanks to Iveta Triskova, Ph.D. (Department of Chemistry, Masaryk University, Brno, Czech Republic); Monika Stupavska, Ph.D. (Department of Physical Electronics, Masaryk University, Brno, Czech Republic); and Filip Mravec, Ph.D. (Laboratory of Biotechnology and Biomaterials Research, Brno University of Technology, Brno, Czech Republic) for their expert assistance with circular dichroism analysis, XPS analysis and time-resolved fluorescence analysis, respectively. This research has been financially supported by the Ministry of Education, Youth and Sports of the Czech Republic under the project CEITEC 2020 (LQ1601).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2017.01.067.
